title: Portugal's AMALIA treats AI as a public good
gist: Portugal launched AMALIA, a publicly funded, open-source 9-billion-parameter Portuguese-language foundation model, framed as an investment in AI sovereignty and treating AI as a public good rather than a US-style private good. The piece argues the US has inverted its historical playbook by privatizing AI's foundational layer while socializing its costs.

themes.theme: Sovereign AI as a third path

insights.claim: Portugal launched AMALIA on July 1, 2026, an open-source AI model built to preserve Portuguese language and culture and reduce dependence on terms set by a handful of US companies.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Signals a growing European 'sovereign AI' investment thesis distinct from US Big Tech and Chinese models; watch for similar public-funded national model programs as a new category of government AI spend.
insights.quote: there is a third path forward being pursued by countries in Europe that view AI as a public, not a private good

insights.claim: AMALIA was built by a consortium coordinated by NOVA University Lisbon and financed with public funds of EUR 5.5 million, plus a further EUR 1.5 million committed through 2027.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The roughly EUR 7 million total shows sovereign foundation models can be attempted at trivial cost relative to frontier labs, lowering the barrier for other governments to follow.
insights.locator: AMALIA is named after Amalia Rodrigues

insights.claim: AMALIA is not intended for direct public use but as a foundational open-source technology that institutions, universities, companies, and researchers can fine-tune, with training conforming to EU data and privacy legislation.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Positioning as infrastructure rather than a consumer product means adoption should be measured by downstream applications built on it, not user counts.
insights.locator: Importantly, the model is open source

insights.claim: At 9 billion parameters, AMALIA is far smaller than the systems behind OpenAI's ChatGPT and Anthropic's Claude, so judged on raw capability alone the project would look like a failure.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Reframes success criteria away from benchmark capability toward autonomy and control, a lens investors should apply when valuing state-backed AI efforts.
insights.quote: If the focus of the project was capability alone, one would be tempted to call it a failure.

insights.claim: An open-source foundation model qualifies as a genuine public good because one party's use does not diminish availability to others and the open license means no gatekeeper can exclude anyone.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The non-rival, non-excludable framing underpins the case for state funding and against relying on closed commercial APIs for critical infrastructure.
insights.quote: A public good is one whose use by one party does not diminish its availability to others and from which no one can easily be excluded.
insights.locator: The public good framing is not a metaphor

insights.claim: Private markets predictably underprovide public goods because builders cannot capture more than a fraction of the benefits, which is why no private developer openly supplied a faithful Portuguese-language model.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Identifies a persistent market gap in low-resource languages that governments, not private labs, are likely to fill, shaping where non-US AI demand and funding flows.

insights.claim: Portuguese is the world's fifth most spoken language with roughly 260 million speakers, yet accounts for only about 2 percent of the web data typically used to train large models.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The mismatch between speaker base and training-data share quantifies the underserved-language opportunity driving national model investments.
insights.locator: That is precisely the pattern visible in language

insights.claim: Where private developers have attempted Portuguese-language models, as in Brazil, the result has been a closed commercial product rather than an open one.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Confirms that market-driven language AI defaults to closed monetization, reinforcing the rationale for public open alternatives.

themes.theme: US inversion of the general-purpose technology playbook

insights.claim: The most capable US AI models are strict private goods with closed inner workings, subscription-gated access, and terms that can be revoked or repriced at will, governed largely by firm self-governance absent comprehensive federal legislation.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Highlights regulatory and pricing-power risk for enterprises dependent on closed US models, and a policy vacuum that could invite future intervention.
insights.quote: what strategy gives, strategy can take away
insights.locator: The United States has organized things the other way around

insights.claim: The US inverted its historical model for general-purpose technologies like the internet, GPS, and modern computing, which government seeded and kept open, by instead keeping AI's foundational layer privately owned and making the public sector line up as just another customer.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Frames the current AI market structure as a historical anomaly, suggesting potential for policy correction toward public provision that could reshape procurement.
insights.locator: This represents a break with how the United States

insights.claim: US data centers receive tax abatements, subsidized land, and priority access to power and water, with grid upgrades partly financed by ordinary ratepayers who also bear noise pollution, water scarcity, and high electricity bills.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: The socialized-input, privatized-output pattern points to rising political and ratepayer backlash risk against data-center buildouts and their subsidies.
insights.quote: The inputs of artificial intelligence are being socialized, while the outputs remain proprietary
insights.locator: The inversion is all the more striking

insights.claim: AMALIA faces familiar public-provision risks: the model aging as the frontier advances, budget shortfalls in a difficult political environment, and users defaulting to other models and thereby starving it of improvements.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: These failure modes are the concrete signposts to monitor when assessing whether any sovereign open-model bet succeeds.
insights.locator: To be sure, public provision carries its own risks

insights.claim: The real test of AMALIA over the next two years is whether anyone actually builds on the foundation, since for roughly EUR 7 million, less than a highway interchange, Portugal bought a public stake in a technology its economy will increasingly run on.
insights.speaker: Monica de Bolle
insights.so_what: Downstream build-on adoption is the key metric; the tiny cost makes the option value attractive and replicable for other small economies.
insights.quote: For roughly €7 million, less than the cost of a highway interchange, Portugal has bought itself a public stake in a technology its economy will increasingly run on.
insights.locator: But the terms of the experiment
